title: MLOps Specialization (Coursera/Board Infinity)
description: 3-course series covering DevOps for ML, cloud platforms for ML (AWS SageMaker, Azure ML, GCP Vertex AI), and model serving systems with A/B testing and latency optimization.

title: LLMOps Specialization (Duke University/Coursera)
description: 6-course series covering generative AI, operationalizing LLMs on Azure, advanced data engineering, GenAI on AWS, Databricks to local LLMs, and open-source LLMOps solutions. 20+ hands-on projects.

title: LLMOps Short Course (DeepLearning.AI)
description: Concise course on supervised fine-tuning pipelines, data/model versioning with BigQuery and Kubeflow, and responsible AI safety scoring. Taught by Google Cloud engineers.

title: Machine Learning in Production (DeepLearning.AI)
description: Andrew Ng course covering the full ML project lifecycle: deployment, concept drift, data pipelines, error analysis, monitoring, and continuous improvement. 11 hours of content.

title: MLOps and LLMOps BootCamp (AgileFever)
description: 6-week live bootcamp with 16 modules covering Python, Git, CI/CD, MLflow, DVC, Docker, Kubernetes, Airflow, Grafana, GCP Vertex AI, RAG, and LLM deployment. Includes 2 capstone projects.

title: LLMOps Masterclass 2026 (Udemy)
description: 17-hour course covering generative AI, prompt engineering, LLM app deployment with FastAPI/Docker/Kubernetes, CI/CD with GitHub Actions, and LLM monitoring with WhyLabs and Langkit.

title: LLMOps: Building Production-Ready LLM Systems (Educative)
description: Interactive 3-hour course covering RAG architecture, prompt management as versioned artifacts, LLM evaluation with LLM-as-a-judge, and production hardening with OWASP security and containerization.

title: MLOps Engineering on AWS (EnterOne)
description: 3-day live course covering MLOps on AWS with SageMaker, Step Functions, SageMaker Pipelines, model monitoring, CI/CD for ML, and the MLOps maturity framework.

title: CI/CD Pipeline for ML Models
description: Step-by-step workflow for setting up continuous integration and deployment for ML models with automated testing, canary deployment, and rollback.
workflow: Set up model registry: MLflow or Weights & Biases for versioning
Create training pipeline: data ingestion, preprocessing, training, evaluation
Write model validation tests: accuracy, latency, fairness, drift detection
Set up GitHub Actions workflow: trigger on model config changes
Build Docker image with model and dependencies
Run integration tests: API endpoint returns correct format and latency
Deploy to staging: Kubernetes canary deployment with 10% traffic
Run A/B test: compare new model vs baseline on real traffic
Promote to production if metrics meet SLOs
Rollback plan: keep previous model version warm for instant rollback

title: Docker + Kubernetes ML Deployment
description: Step-by-step workflow for deploying ML models with Docker containers and Kubernetes orchestration including autoscaling and health checks.
workflow: Create model serving API with FastAPI or BentoML
Write Dockerfile: FROM python:3.12-slim, install deps, copy model and API
Build and test locally: docker build -t my-model . && docker run -p 8000:8000 my-model
Push to container registry: docker push registry/my-model:v1
Create Kubernetes Deployment: replicas, resource limits, GPU requests
Create Kubernetes Service: ClusterIP for internal, LoadBalancer for external
Add Horizontal Pod Autoscaler: scale based on CPU/memory/custom metrics
Configure liveness and readiness probes for model health checks
Set up rolling update strategy: maxSurge=1, maxUnavailable=0
Monitor with Prometheus: model latency, throughput, error rate metrics

title: Model Monitoring and Alerting Setup
description: Set up production model monitoring with drift detection, Prometheus metrics, and Grafana alerting for ML models.
workflow: Deploy MLflow or W&B for experiment tracking and model registry
Implement prediction logging: log inputs, outputs, timestamps, model version
Set up feature store: Feast for feature serving and monitoring
Configure data drift detection: Evidently with KS test or PSI
Configure concept drift detection: monitor prediction accuracy over time
Set up Prometheus metrics: request latency P50/P95/P99, error rate, throughput
Create Grafana dashboards: model performance, feature distributions, drift scores
Configure alerting rules: alert on drift score > threshold or accuracy drop > 5%
Implement shadow deployment: run new model in parallel without serving traffic
Set up retraining trigger: auto-retrain when drift exceeds threshold

title: Feature Store Setup with Feast
description: Step-by-step workflow for setting up a feature store with Feast for ML feature management, online serving, and training-serving consistency.
workflow: Install Feast: pip install feast
Create feature repository: feast init my_feature_repo
Define entity and feature views in feature_definitions.py
Set up offline store: BigQuery, Snowflake, or Redshift for training data
Set up online store: Redis or DynamoDB for low-latency serving
Register feature definitions: feast apply
Materialize features: feast materialize to populate online store
Build training dataset: feast.get_historical_features for model training
Serve features in production: feast.get_online_features for real-time inference
Monitor feature drift: compare online vs offline feature statistics

title: ML Model Testing Pipeline
description: Comprehensive testing pipeline for ML models before production deployment covering unit, integration, performance, fairness, and robustness tests.
workflow: Unit tests: test data preprocessing functions with known inputs/outputs
Integration tests: test full pipeline end-to-end with sample data
Model performance tests: verify accuracy > threshold on held-out test set
Latency tests: measure P50, P95, P99 inference latency with production load
Memory tests: verify model fits in GPU/CPU memory with production batch size
Fairness tests: check model performance across demographic groups
Robustness tests: test with adversarial inputs, edge cases, missing data
Data drift tests: compare input feature distributions with training data
Schema validation: verify input schema matches expected format
Smoke test: deploy to staging and verify health endpoint responds

title: Canary Deployment with KServe and Prometheus
description: Progressive canary rollout for ML models on Kubernetes using KServe traffic splitting, Prometheus metrics, and automated rollback based on error rate and latency thresholds.
workflow: Deploy baseline InferenceService with 100% traffic on KServe
Create canary InferenceService with new model version
Set canaryTrafficPercent to 5 for initial canary rollout
Configure Prometheus ServiceMonitor to scrape prediction latency, errors, confidence per model version
Add Prometheus alert rules for canary error rate > 1%, latency P99 > threshold, confidence drop > 5%
Implement automated canary controller: progressively shift traffic 5-10-25-50-75-100%
Monitor Grafana dashboards comparing baseline vs canary metrics
On alert: automatically rollback by setting canaryTrafficPercent to 0
On success: promote canary by removing canaryTrafficPercent field
Tag-based A/B routing for explicit experiment URLs alongside canary

title: MLflow 3 Experiment Tracking and GenAI Evaluation
description: Python code for tracking ML experiments, registering models, and evaluating LLM outputs using MLflow 3 genai evaluation with built-in scorers.
code:
import mlflow
from mlflow.genai import evaluate, make_judge
from mlflow.genai.scorers import Correctness, Safety, RelevanceToQuery

# Set tracking URI and experiment
mlflow.set_tracking_uri("http://127.0.0.1:5000")
mlflow.set_experiment("fraud-detection")

# Train and log model
with mlflow.start_run(run_name="rf-v1"):
    mlflow.log_param("n_estimators", 100)
    mlflow.log_param("max_depth", 10)

    model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, max_depth=10)
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)

    accuracy = model.score(X_test, y_test)
    mlflow.log_metric("accuracy", accuracy)
    mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, "model")

# Register model
mlflow.register_model(
    f"runs:/{mlflow.active_run().info.run_id}/model",
    "fraud-detection-model"
)

# Evaluate LLM outputs with built-in scorers
judge = make_judge(model="openai://gpt-4o")
result = evaluate(
    data=eval_df,
    scorers=[Correctness(), Safety(), RelevanceToQuery()],
    judge=judge
)

title: FastAPI Model Serving with Health Checks and Metrics
description: Production FastAPI server for ML model serving with health checks, Prometheus metrics, and structured response format.
code:
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram, generate_latest
import time

app = FastAPI(title="ML Model Server", version="1.0.0")
model = None
model_version = "v1.0.0"

PREDICTION_COUNT = Counter("predictions_total", "Total predictions")
PREDICTION_LATENCY = Histogram("prediction_latency_seconds", "Prediction latency")

class PredictionRequest(BaseModel):
    features: list[float]

class PredictionResponse(BaseModel):
    prediction: float
    probability: float
    model_version: str
    latency_ms: float

@app.on_event("startup")
async def load_model():
    global model
    model = load_model_from_registry()

@app.get("/health")
async def health():
    return {"status": "healthy", "model_version": model_version}

@app.post("/predict", response_model=PredictionResponse)
async def predict(request: PredictionRequest):
    start = time.time()
    result = model.predict([request.features])
    latency = (time.time() - start) * 1000
    PREDICTION_COUNT.inc()
    PREDICTION_LATENCY.observe(latency / 1000)
    return PredictionResponse(
        prediction=float(result[0]),
        probability=float(result[1]),
        model_version=model_version,
        latency_ms=latency
    )

@app.get("/metrics")
async def metrics():
    return Response(generate_latest(), media_type="text/plain")

title: Docker Compose for ML Stack with MLflow, Redis, Prometheus, and Grafana
description: Docker Compose configuration for a complete ML serving stack with model server, feature store, monitoring, and model registry.
code:
services:
  model-server:
    build: ./model-server
    ports: ["127.0.0.1:8000:8000"]
    environment:
      MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI: http://mlflow:5000
      FEAST_ONLINE_URL: redis://redis:6379
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-sf", "http://127.0.0.1:8000/health"]
      interval: 30s
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: all
              capabilities: [gpu]

  mlflow:
    image: ghcr.io/mlflow/mlflow:v2.22.0
    ports: ["127.0.0.1:5000:5000"]
    environment:
      MLFLOW_BACKEND_STORE_URI: postgresql://mlflow:mlflow@db:5432/mlflow

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports: ["127.0.0.1:6379:6379"]

  prometheus:
    image: prom/prometheus
    ports: ["127.0.0.1:9090:9090"]

  grafana:
    image: grafana/grafana
    ports: ["127.0.0.1:3000:3000"]

title: GitHub Actions CI/CD for ML Models
description: GitHub Actions workflow for automated ML model training, testing, validation, and Kubernetes deployment.
code:
name: ML Pipeline
on:
  push:
    paths: ["model/**", "data/**"]

jobs:
  train-and-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: "3.12"}
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: python train.py
      - run: pytest tests/ -v
      - run: python validate_model.py --threshold 0.95
      - name: Deploy to staging
        if: success()
        run: |
          docker build -t model-server:${{ github.sha }} .
          docker push registry/model-server:${{ github.sha }}
          kubectl set image deployment/model-server \
            model-server=registry/model-server:${{ github.sha }} \
            --namespace=staging

title: Data Drift Detection with Evidently
description: Python code for detecting data and concept drift in production ML models using Evidently with PSI and KS test statistical methods.
code:
from evidently import ColumnMapping
from evidently.report import Report
from evidently.metric_preset import DataDriftPreset, RegressionPreset
import pandas as pd

reference_data = pd.read_parquet("data/train.parquet")
current_data = pd.read_parquet("data/production_2026_05.parquet")

column_mapping = ColumnMapping(
    target="target",
    prediction="prediction",
    numerical_features=["age", "income", "score"],
    categorical_features=["category", "region"]
)

drift_report = Report(metrics=[
    DataDriftPreset(),
    RegressionPreset()
])
drift_report.run(
    reference_data=reference_data,
    current_data=current_data,
    column_mapping=column_mapping
)

drift_result = drift_report.as_dict()
if drift_result["data_drift"]["share"] > 0.3:
    print("WARNING: Data drift detected! Retraining recommended.")
else:
    print("No significant drift detected. Model is healthy.")

title: A/B Testing with Consistent Hash-Based Routing
description: Python implementation of hash-based A/B traffic splitting for ML models with deterministic user assignment and statistical significance testing.
code:
import hashlib
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats

def get_variant(user_id: str, variants: dict[str, float]) -> str:
    """Deterministic hash-based variant assignment."""
    hash_val = int(hashlib.md5(user_id.encode()).hexdigest(), 16)
    bucket = (hash_val % 10000) / 10000.0
    cumulative = 0.0
    for variant, weight in variants.items():
        cumulative += weight
        if bucket < cumulative:
            return variant
    return list(variants.keys())[-1]

def analyze_ab_test(
    control: np.ndarray, treatment: np.ndarray, alpha: float = 0.05
) -> dict:
    """Welch's t-test for A/B test significance."""
    t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_ind(control, treatment, equal_var=False)
    ci = stats.t.interval(
        0.95,
        df=len(control) + len(treatment) - 2,
        loc=np.mean(treatment) - np.mean(control),
        scale=stats.sem(treatment - control)
    )
    return {
        "significant": p_value < alpha,
        "p_value": p_value,
        "effect_size": np.mean(treatment) - np.mean(control),
        "confidence_interval": ci
    }

title: GPU Scheduling with Kubernetes DRA
description: Kubernetes manifest for GPU workload scheduling using Dynamic Resource Allocation with time-slicing and MIG partitioning for cost optimization.
code:
apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: ResourceClaimTemplate
metadata:
  name: gpu-claim-template
spec:
  spec:
    devices:
      requests:
        - name: gpu
          deviceClassName: nvidia.com/gpu
          allocationMode: ExactCount
          count: 1
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: ml-training
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels: {app: ml-training}
  template:
    metadata:
      labels: {app: ml-training}
    spec:
      resourceClaims:
        - name: gpu-claim
          resourceClaimTemplateName: gpu-claim-template
      containers:
        - name: trainer
          image: ml-training:v1
          resources:
            claims:
              - name: gpu-claim
          env:
            - name: NVIDIA_VISIBLE_DEVICES
              value: "all"
          volumeMounts:
            - name: model-output
              mountPath: /output
      volumes:
        - name: model-output
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: model-pvc

title: MLflow
description: Open-source platform for the complete ML lifecycle: experiment tracking, model registry, model serving, AI Gateway for LLMs, prompt management, tracing, and genai evaluation. Version 3.x adds LLM judge scorers and budget alerts.

title: Kubeflow
description: Kubernetes-native ML platform for managing ML workflows: pipelines (KFP v2), training operators, hyperparameter tuning (Katib), model serving (KServe), and notebook servers. Best for teams already on Kubernetes.

title: Weights & Biases (W&B)
description: Experiment tracking, dataset versioning, hyperparameter sweeps, model evaluation, and collaborative dashboards. W&B Weave adds LLM observability with tracing and structured evaluation.

title: Evidently
description: Open-source ML monitoring and LLM observability with 100+ built-in metrics for data drift, data quality, model performance, embeddings drift, and text descriptors. Integrates with Prometheus, Grafana, and MLflow.

title: Feast
description: Open-source feature store for ML: online serving (Redis/DynamoDB) for real-time inference, offline computing (BigQuery/Snowflake) for training. Prevents training-serving skew. Kubernetes-native deployment with RBAC and HPA.

title: BentoML
description: Production-grade Python framework for building and deploying AI/ML model inference APIs. Handles adaptive batching, multi-model composition, GPU scheduling, and auto-generates Docker images. Supports LLM serving via vLLM.

title: DVC (Data Version Control)
description: Git-like version control for data and ML models. Tracks data files, models, and metrics alongside code. Enables reproducible experiments, data pipelines (dvc.yaml), and collaboration via DVC Studio.

title: KitOps
description: CNCF Sandbox tool for packaging, versioning, and sharing AI/ML models as OCI artifacts (ModelKit format). Tamper-proof with SHA-256 digests, signable with Cosign, compatible with all major OCI registries. Integrates with MLflow, KServe, and Kubeflow.

title: KServe
description: Kubernetes-native model serving with first-class LLM support (v0.15+). Provides canary deployments, autoscaling (KEDA), scale-to-zero, GPU time-slicing, and multi-framework inference (vLLM, Triton, MLServer). Standard inference protocol V2.

title: Shadow Deployment Pattern
description: Deploy a new model version alongside the production model. Route real traffic to the production model but also send copies to the shadow model. Compare outputs without affecting users. Promote when shadow model meets SLOs.

title: Feature Store Pattern
description: Centralize feature computation and serving. Train on offline features (batch), serve from online store (low-latency). Prevents training-serving skew by using the same feature definitions for both. Feast is the canonical open-source implementation.

title: Canary Deployment Pattern
description: Route a small percentage (1-5%) of production traffic to a new model version. Monitor metrics (latency, accuracy, error rate). Gradually increase traffic if metrics are good. Rollback instantly if issues detected. KServe provides native canary support.

title: Model Registry Pattern
description: Centralize model versioning, metadata, and lifecycle management. Track model versions, stages (staging/production/archived), and lineage. MLflow Model Registry is the standard open-source implementation with GenAI prompt registry in v3+.

title: Data Pipeline Pattern (Medallion Architecture)
description: Organize data in three layers: Bronze (raw ingestion), Silver (cleaned, deduplicated), Gold (feature-engineered, ready for ML). Enables incremental processing and clear data lineage. Standard pattern with Great Expectations validation at each boundary.

title: Infrastructure as Code for ML Platforms
description: Manage ML infrastructure (GPU clusters, feature stores, model serving endpoints) with Terraform or Pulumi. Define environments as code for reproducible, auditable deployments. Use Policy-as-Code (OPA) for cost governance and security compliance before apply.

title: GPU Cost Optimization Pattern
description: Reduce GPU costs through MIG partitioning (7 isolated instances per A100), time-slicing for dev/test, gang scheduling (Volcano) for distributed training, and autoscaling (KEDA) for inference. Use OpenCost for GPU cost attribution and HAMi for GPU virtualization.

title: MLflow GenAI Tutorial Series
description: 12 step-by-step notebooks covering MLflow 3 GenAI: experiment tracking, tracing, prompt management, framework integrations (OpenAI, LangChain, LlamaIndex), agent evaluation, prompt optimization (GEPA), RAG, and multi-agent patterns.

title: MLflow Official Quickstart
description: Official quickstart for MLflow: install, track experiments, register models, and serve models with the MLflow tracking server. Covers both classical ML and GenAI workflows.

title: Kubeflow Pipelines V2 on Kubernetes
description: End-to-end tutorial for building ML pipelines with Kubeflow Pipelines V2 SDK: typed Input/Output artifacts, data validation with Great Expectations, multi-model training, conditional execution, and KServe model deployment.

title: DVC Getting Started Guide
description: Official DVC tutorial covering data versioning, pipeline definitions (dvc.yaml), experiment tracking with DVCLive, and collaboration with Git and DVC remote storage.

title: Canary Model Rollouts with KServe and Prometheus
description: End-to-end tutorial for canary deployments with KServe traffic splitting, Prometheus alert rules for error rate and latency, automated progressive traffic shifting (5-10-25-50-75-100%), and rollback based on metric thresholds.

title: ML Model Serving with BentoML and Kubernetes
description: Comprehensive guide for packaging ML models with BentoML, auto-generating Docker images, deploying to Kubernetes with HPA autoscaling, and serving LLMs via vLLM with GPU acceleration.

title: IaC Patterns for ML Platforms (Terraform)
description: 6 major infrastructure-as-code patterns for ML: platform modules, blue-green infrastructure deployment, canary infrastructure with weighted DNS, self-destructing GPU experiments, OPA policy-as-code, and GPU quota management.

title: Machine Learning in Production (Christian Kastner)
description: Practical 616-page textbook from CMU covering the full ML system lifecycle: requirements, design, quality assurance, operations, and responsible AI. Based on the popular CMU 17-645 course. Released under Creative Commons license.

title: Machine Learning Platform Engineering (Manning)
description: Shows how to build an internal developer platform for ML and AI systems. Covers Kubeflow, MLflow, BentoML, Evidently, Feast, and LangChain for end-to-end MLOps/LLMOps platform setup.

title: Machine Learning Engineering on AWS (2nd Edition)
description: Practical guide to building production-ready ML systems on AWS with Amazon Bedrock, next-gen SageMaker, RAG-powered GenAI, LLMOps workflows, AI agents, model deployment, monitoring, cost optimization, and security compliance.

title: Designing Machine Learning Systems (Chip Huyen)
description: The canonical MLOps reference covering ML system design, data management, feature engineering, model evaluation, deployment patterns, monitoring, and continuous learning. Accompanied by a curated MLOps tools list on GitHub.

title: AI Engineering (Chip Huyen)
description: 2025 follow-up to Designing ML Systems, focused on building applications with foundation models. Covers the AI landscape, LLM evaluation, prompt engineering, RAG, fine-tuning, and efficient deployment of GenAI systems.

title: Machine Learning Engineering in Action (Ben Wilson)
description: Hands-on guide to ML engineering patterns: feature stores, CI/CD for ML, model testing, deployment strategies, and monitoring. Written by MLflow maintainers with production-tested patterns.

title: MLOpsLab.org LLMOps Crash Course: Beginners Guide to LLM Operations
description: Free crash course covering the LLM operations lifecycle and workflow: data pipelines, model training, deployment, monitoring, and scaling. Designed for engineers new to LLMOps.

title: MLOps and LLMOps: Deploying and Scaling AI in Production (Coursera/Board Infinity)
description: Coursera course covering MLOps and LLMOps fundamentals, CI/CD for ML, model deployment, monitoring, scaling, and production best practices for both traditional ML and LLM workloads.

title: PromptTools
description: Open-source tools for prompt testing and experimentation with support for LLMs and vector databases. 3k+ stars. Provides systematic prompt evaluation, comparison, and regression testing.

title: Vector Admin
description: Universal tool suite for vector database management. 2.2k+ stars. Manage Pinecone, Chroma, Qdrant, Weaviate, and more vector databases from a single interface.

title: Awesome MLOps Contents
description: Comprehensive curated collection of DataOps and MLOps contents. 132+ stars. Covers courses, books, blogs, tools, and best practices for ML operations.

title: Rocketride Server
description: High-performance AI pipeline engine with C++ core and 50+ Python-extensible nodes. 3.3k+ stars. Build, debug, and scale LLM workflows with 13+ model providers, 8+ vector databases, and agent orchestration.
